name: Waikato
history: The Waikato region has been home to Maori peoples for centuries, with the Tainui iwi establishing deep roots in this fertile river valley. European settlement accelerated in the 19th century, particularly after the Waikato Wars of the 1860s, which reshaped the region's political and social landscape. The construction of the Waikato River hydroelectric dams in the 20th century transformed the area into a major source of New Zealand's power supply. Today, the region is known for its rich agricultural heritage, dairy farming, and thriving food production industries.
culturalNotes: Waikato is defined by its agricultural identity and strong connection to the land, particularly through dairy farming and pastoral traditions that have shaped the region for generations. The area maintains significant Maori cultural presence and influence, with numerous marae serving as community centers and venues for celebrating iwi heritage. The region is also famous for its equestrian traditions and rural lifestyle, reflected in its farming practices and local festivals that celebrate rural New Zealand culture.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit from October to April for warm weather and outdoor activities.
languageNotes: Te Reo Maori is increasingly present in the region, with many place names and community initiatives reflecting Maori language revival. English is the primary language spoken, though visitors may encounter Maori words and phrases in daily contexts, particularly around cultural sites and events.

places.name: Hamilton
places.description: Waikato's largest city, Hamilton is a vibrant cultural hub with excellent museums, gardens, and riverside attractions. It's the gateway to the region and offers quality accommodation, dining, and shopping alongside fascinating Maori heritage experiences.

activities.detail: Explore New Zealand's largest regional museum featuring Maori taonga, natural history, and contemporary art across multiple galleries.
Wander through 54 hectares of themed gardens including the stunning Paradise Garden and Indian Garden with seasonal plantings and water features.
Stroll along the scenic Waikato River walkway connecting gardens, parks, and cafes with views of the city and native birdlife.
Visit this riverside reserve featuring heritage trees, walking tracks, and picnic areas in a peaceful natural setting.
Discover vibrant murals and street art installations throughout the city, particularly concentrated in the CBD and Hunter Street areas.

activities.text: Momentum New Zealand Mountain Bike Park
activities.detail: Experience world-class mountain biking trails suitable for all skill levels just outside the city.

food.detail: Sample premium grass-fed beef from local farms at quality restaurants, known for its rich flavor and tenderness.
Taste the region's signature feijoa fruit in desserts, smoothies, and specialty foods during autumn season.
Experience traditional earth-oven cooked meats and vegetables, a culturally significant meal preparation method available at various venues.
Try locally caught eel and trout prepared in contemporary restaurants celebrating Waikato's waterways.
Visit weekend markets for fresh local produce, artisan goods, and street food from regional producers.

culture.detail: Hamilton is on Maori land (Kirikiriroa); acknowledge this in conversations and support Maori-owned businesses and cultural experiences.
The Waikato River is central to local identity and Maori culture; take time to visit and respect this taonga (treasure).
Plan visits around the city's events like the Waikato River trails festival and summer riverside concerts that celebrate community culture.
Learn and use the Maori name Kirikiriroa alongside Hamilton; the dual naming reflects cultural recognition and local pride.

places.name: Rotorua
places.description: Famous for its geothermal wonders, Rotorua features bubbling mud pools, hot springs, and colorful geysers set against a dramatic volcanic landscape. It's also the cultural heart of Maori New Zealand with authentic experiences and traditional performances.

activities.detail: Witness the spectacular Pohutu Geyser erupting up to 30 meters high and explore bubbling mud pools and colorful hot springs.
Walk suspended bridges through a 140-year-old California redwood forest canopy with stunning views over the geothermal landscape.
Paddle across the pristine lake surrounded by geothermal vents and volcanic scenery, with options for guided tours.
Explore Maori taonga (treasures) and volcanic history in the historic 1908 Tudor-style bathhouse building.
Experience an authentic reconstructed pre-European Maori village with traditional performances, hangi feast, and cultural demonstrations.
Explore colorful geothermal features including the Champagne Pool with its vivid orange and yellow mineral deposits.
Walk through the excavated remains of a Maori village buried by the 1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera.

food.detail: A traditional earth oven-cooked feast featuring meat, vegetables, and kumara (sweet potato) that is central to Maori dining culture.
Fresh eggs boiled naturally in the hot springs around Rotorua, offering a unique experience of the geothermal landscape.
A New Zealand-style sourdough bread traditionally made with native Maori potatoes, commonly served at local cafes and restaurants.
Freshly caught trout and char from the pristine lake, often smoked or pan-fried at local establishments.
Local fusion pizzas inspired by Maori traditions, blending contemporary and indigenous flavors unique to Rotorua eateries.

culture.detail: Greet locals with 'Kia ora' and familiarize yourself with Maori words like 'tapu' (sacred) and 'mana' (prestige), which are integral to understanding Rotorua's identity.
When visiting a traditional marae (communal gathering place), always remove your shoes as a sign of respect for the sacred space.
Stay on marked paths around hot springs and geysers, as many areas are considered tapu and departing from trails can be dangerous and culturally disrespectful.

culture.text: Attend a Traditional Hangi and Performance
culture.detail: Participating in a hangi feast accompanied by haka performances is not just tourism but an opportunity to understand Maori hospitality and cultural expression.

places.name: Matamata
places.description: This charming rural town is best known as the home of the Hobbiton Movie Set, where The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit were filmed. The meticulously preserved film set offers an immersive journey into Middle-earth.

activities.detail: Explore the fully preserved film set of the Shire with guided tours through hobbit holes, gardens, and the Green Dragon Inn.
Climb this historic 1881 stone tower on a hill for panoramic views across the Matamata countryside and farmland.
Visit the central information hub for local advice, maps, and booking assistance for attractions and activities in the region.
Relax in naturally heated mineral pools and spa facilities located just outside town, drawing geothermal water from beneath the region.
Explore Māori taonga, local heritage, and exhibits about the Waikato region's history and culture, located 30 minutes south.
Play an 18-hole championship course set against the backdrop of the Kainai Mountains on rolling pastoral land.

food.detail: Sample premium New Zealand beef from the surrounding farmland, served at restaurants throughout town and known for its quality and flavour.

food.text: Hobbiton-inspired meals at The Green Dragon
food.detail: Dine at the replica pub within the film set, serving traditional comfort food and ales in an authentic Middle-earth setting.
Purchase or taste locally made cheeses, butter, and yoghurt from nearby dairy farms that offer farm shop experiences.
Try locally raised lamb dishes at regional restaurants, a staple protein of the fertile farming region surrounding Matamata.

culture.text: Book Hobbiton tours well in advance
culture.detail: Tours operate year-round but sell out quickly during peak seasons; advance online booking is essential for guaranteed entry.

culture.text: Respect farm property and rural privacy
culture.detail: The region is active farmland; stay on designated paths and roads, and avoid trespassing on private properties beyond tourist attractions.
The Waikato region experiences variable weather year-round; bring layers and rain protection even on seemingly clear days.
Matamata is in open countryside; allocate extra travel time to attractions and bring cash as some remote farm venues may lack card facilities.

places.name: Cambridge
places.description: An elegant thoroughbred horse racing town with tree-lined streets, upscale shops, and excellent cafes, Cambridge offers a more refined countryside experience. It's known for its pastoral charm and proximity to prestigious studs and equestrian facilities.

activities.detail: Explore New Zealand's premier equestrian museum housed in a historic building, showcasing the region's world-renowned bloodstock breeding heritage and racing history.
Visit prestigious thoroughbred studs like Cambridge Stud or Matamata studs in the surrounding district to see champion racehorses and learn about breeding operations.
Stroll through Cambridge's elegant tree-lined streets featuring Victorian and Edwardian architecture, boutique shops, and galleries clustered around the Main Street precinct.
Enjoy water sports, fishing, and scenic walks around this popular lake just 15 minutes south of Cambridge with picnic areas and boat ramps.
Visit this charming 1870s cottage that exemplifies Cambridge's colonial heritage and offers insight into early settler life in the Waikato.
Located in nearby Hamilton, this regional museum displays Māori treasures and local history relevant to the broader Waikato district including Cambridge's racing culture.

food.detail: Sample premium milk, cheese, and yoghurt from Waikato dairy farms sold in Cambridge shops, reflecting the region's pastoral heritage and agricultural excellence.
Enjoy tender lamb dishes at local restaurants, sourced from the region's renowned sheep stations and prepared to showcase the meat's exceptional quality.
Visit Cambridge's upscale cafes along Main Street for expertly crafted coffee and fresh brunch offerings in elegant settings favored by locals and equestrian visitors.
Dine at restaurants featuring seasonal produce from Waikato's fertile agricultural region, often accompanied by local wines and craft beverages.
Try iconic New Zealand meat pies from local bakeries, a countryside staple popular among racing day crowds and town visitors.

culture.detail: Plan visits around Cambridge's horse racing events and the wider racing season, as the town's social calendar revolves significantly around equestrian activities and racing days.
Respect the refined, country-style dress culture of Cambridge—locals favor smart-casual and equestrian-inspired fashion, particularly visible during racing events and town social gatherings.
Book tours in advance and follow farm protocols when visiting studs, as these are working breeding operations with specific visiting hours and photography restrictions to protect valuable horses.
Recognize that the Waikato region holds significant Māori history; Cambridge was built on Tainui lands, and many local place names and cultural sites reflect this heritage.

places.name: Te Awamutu
places.description: A historic town home to the impressive Te Awamutu Museum, which houses significant Maori artifacts and taonga. The surrounding area provides access to beautiful native bush walks and the Te Awamutu Caves.

activities.detail: Explore the region's largest collection of Maori taonga and artifacts, including treasured greenstone and traditional weapons in this award-winning museum.
Walk through illuminated underground caverns featuring stalactites, stalagmites, and a glowworm grotto accessed via a short bush track.
Venture into native beech and podocarp forest with multiple walking trails ranging from easy riverside walks to challenging ridge-top hikes.
A scenic 3.5-kilometre loop walk through native bush leading to cascading waterfall pools suitable for swimming.
Collect detailed information about local walks, Maori heritage sites, and community events while viewing regional displays.
Visit this sacred Maori burial ground and historical site to understand the area's deeper cultural significance and landscape history.

food.detail: Take advantage of Waikato's renowned pastoral farming with restaurant dishes featuring premium local beef and lamb raised in the region's fertile farmland.
Sample cheese and ice cream made from Waikato's famous dairy herds at local cafes and specialist food shops throughout town.
Try this traditional Maori bread made with a natural sourdough starter, available at local bakeries and farmers markets.
Experience this regional delicacy prepared traditionally or in contemporary style, sourced from local waterways and streams.
Look for dishes incorporating pickled or cooked bracken fern, a traditional Maori bush food featured in heritage cuisine experiences.

culture.detail: Many locations around Te Awamutu hold deep spiritual significance to iwi Maori; always follow signage and seek permission before entering restricted areas.
Learn basic Maori pronunciation for place names like 'Te Awamutu' (te ah-wah-MOO-too) and 'Pureora' to show respect for local culture.
Plan your visit around community events like the Te Awamutu Festival in February or Maori-focused cultural celebrations to experience authentic local engagement.
The town and surrounding region are deeply connected to walking trails and outdoor exploration; embrace this by wearing appropriate footwear and respecting conservation areas.

places.name: Waitomo Caves
places.description: A spectacular limestone cave system famous for its glowworms that illuminate the underground darkness like a starry night sky. Visitors can explore the caves by boat or on foot through awe-inspiring underground chambers.

activities.detail: Take a guided boat ride through the main Glowworm Cave to see thousands of bioluminescent glowworms illuminating the cavern ceiling like stars.
Explore this dry cave system on foot through spectacular chambers filled with stalactites, stalagmites, and flowstone formations without boat access.
Walk through native bush and alongside the Waipa River in this reserve that features a natural cave entrance and scenic forest trails.
Learn about the local Māori heritage, the history of cave tourism, and geology exhibits that explain the formation of these limestone caves.
Experience a more adventurous caving expedition through narrower passages and underground streams in this less-visited cave system.
Browse local Māori artwork and crafts while enjoying refreshments in a cultural space celebrating the region's indigenous heritage.

food.detail: Sample traditional Māori earth-oven roasted meats and root vegetables, a centuries-old cooking method still used in local restaurants and cultural experiences.
Taste locally-made cheese, yogurt, and ice cream from Waikato's renowned dairy farming region, available at local cafes and shops.
Try this seasonal delicacy of tiny freshwater fish lightly battered and pan-fried, a regional specialty particularly available in spring months.
Enjoy locally-sourced venison and wild game in hearty pies sold at village bakeries, reflecting the region's hunting and farming heritage.
Savor Waikato's fresh fruit in desserts and pavlovas featuring feijoas and berries, popular at local cafes during fruit season.

culture.detail: The caves hold deep cultural importance to Tainui Māori; listen respectfully to guides' explanations of iwi history and spiritual connections to the caves.
Waitomo's cave tours operate on schedules and group sizes are limited to preserve the environment; booking ahead ensures access and better experiences.
Cave tours involve wet, slippery surfaces and uneven terrain; sturdy, closed-toe footwear with good grip is essential for safety and comfort.
Many cave areas restrict flash photography to protect the glowworms; follow guide instructions and respect these conservation measures during your visit.

places.name: Hobbiton Movie Set
places.description: The fully functional film set from The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit trilogies, preserved on a working farm near Matamata. Visitors can walk through Hobbiton village, visit the Green Dragon Inn, and experience the magic of Middle-earth firsthand.

activities.detail: Step inside Bilbo's iconic hobbit-hole and explore the meticulously preserved interior with period furnishings and authentic set details.
Visit the working tavern where you can order a pint of ale or cider and enjoy refreshments in the same setting used during filming.
Stroll through the 12-acre village passing 44 hobbit holes, the Mill, and other Middle-earth structures nestled into the rolling Waikato farmland.
Visit the iconic Party Tree where Bilbo's birthday celebration took place and the working water mill that anchors the village center.
Capture memorable photos at carefully designed viewpoints throughout the set, including bridges, gardens, and character homes.
Join a knowledgeable guide who shares behind-the-scenes stories, filming anecdotes, and details about the set construction and preservation.
Browse and send postcards from the movie set's working post office, a unique way to share your visit with friends and family.

food.detail: A specially brewed ale served at the Green Dragon Inn that captures the spirit of hobbit refreshment culture from the films.
The inn offers a curated selection of New Zealand craft beers and ciders paired with the immersive Middle-earth dining experience.
The Green Dragon serves hearty pub fare including meat pies, fish and chips, and comfort foods that reflect the hobbit love of good eating.
Specialty drinks inspired by Middle-earth lore are crafted to enhance the authentic atmosphere of the Shire tavern experience.

culture.detail: Hobbiton operates on a timed-ticket system with guided tours only, so reservations are essential especially during peak summer seasons.
The movie set remains on an active farm, so visitors should stay on marked paths and be mindful of the surrounding agricultural environment and livestock.
The Waikato region experiences changeable weather; bring layers and waterproof jackets as the open hillside location offers little shelter from rain or wind.
Plan for a full morning or afternoon visit to fully experience the village, participate in guided commentary, and enjoy the Green Dragon without rushing.

places.name: Raglan
places.description: A laid-back coastal village known for its excellent black sand beach and strong surf culture, Raglan is perfect for swimming, surfing, and beach walks. The small town maintains a bohemian vibe with local cafes and accommodation.

activities.detail: Raglan's main black sand beach offers consistent left-hand breaks ideal for surfers of all levels, with several local surf schools providing lessons.
A scenic coastal walk from the town to the twin headlands offering panoramic views of the coast and access to more sheltered swimming spots.
Visit the remnants of a pre-European Māori pā (fortified settlement) located on a hilltop overlooking the township with cultural interpretation.

activities.text: Visit local art galleries and craft shops
activities.detail: Browse independent galleries and studios along the main street showcasing local artists, pottery, and bohemian-style crafts reflecting the village's creative community.
Paddle through the calm waters of the sheltered harbour to observe native birdlife and explore the estuary environment.

food.text: Fish and chips from local takeaways
food.detail: Fresh catch prepared with crispy batter and served alongside hand-cut fries, using fish landed from nearby waters.
Raglan's bohemian cafes serve expertly made coffee and locally roasted beans, often paired with homemade pastries and cabinet food.
Small eateries feature seasonal menus highlighting Waikato produce, craft beers, and locally sourced ingredients reflecting the region's agricultural heritage.
Regular local markets offer fresh vegetables, artisanal breads, and handmade products from regional growers and producers.

culture.detail: Be respectful when visiting historical pā sites and ask locals about their significance; Raglan has important Māori heritage that shaped the region.
The village operates on relaxed timings with many shops closing early or taking leisurely hours, so plan accordingly and don't expect urban convenience.

culture.text: Check tides and conditions before beach visits
culture.detail: The black sand beach and harbour environment are strongly influenced by tides; locals and surf schools can advise on safe swimming and surfing windows.
The community values visitors who shop at independent cafes, galleries, and accommodations rather than chains, helping sustain the village's creative character.

places.name: Ngaruawahia
places.description: A historic Maori settlement at the confluence of the Waikato and Waipa rivers, Ngaruawahia is significant for its cultural heritage and as the location of the annual Regatta Festival. The town offers insights into Maori history and riverside recreation.

activities.detail: Attend New Zealand's oldest river regatta held annually on the Waikato River, featuring waka ama racing, traditional Māori canoes, and cultural performances.
Tour this 1880s Victorian mansion set on riverfront grounds to explore colonial-era architecture and local heritage.
Walk or cycle along scenic riverside paths where the Waikato and Waipa rivers converge, offering views of native bush and bird life.
Climb this sacred Māori mountain overlooking Ngaruawahia for panoramic views of the Waikato region and insight into Waikato iwi significance.
Enjoy riverside picnicking and swimming in designated areas along the Waikato River with playground facilities and parking.
Discover Waikato iwi history, traditional artifacts, and contemporary Māori art in this community-focused museum.

food.detail: Sample this traditional Māori sourdough made with a potato-based starter, widely available at local bakeries and a staple of regional cuisine.

food.text: Fresh Eel from the Waikato River
food.detail: Try locally caught tuna (eel), traditionally smoked or grilled, reflecting the region's strong fishing heritage and Māori kai practices.
Purchase award-winning local cheese and butter from the Waikato region, known as New Zealand's dairy heartland.
Experience traditional earth-oven cooked food at local venues or community events, featuring meat, vegetables, and kumara cooked underground.
Visit riverside takeaway shops for fresh fish caught from the Waikato and Waipa rivers, a casual local favorite.

culture.detail: Respect local Māori culture by learning pronunciation of place names like Ngaruawahia and Taupiri, which have deep meaning to Waikato iwi.
Taupiri Mountain and riverside areas are spiritually significant to local Māori; avoid disruptive behavior and follow any posted guidance at culturally important locations.
Plan visits around the Regatta Festival (March) and other iwi events to experience authentic Waikato Māori traditions and community gatherings.
Hire Māori guides or visit iwi-operated attractions to gain genuine insights into the region's history and avoid cultural appropriation.

places.name: Taupo
places.description: Situated on New Zealand's largest lake, Taupo is a premier adventure and outdoor recreation destination offering water sports, fishing, hiking, and geothermal attractions. The lake itself is surrounded by stunning scenery and excellent accommodation options.

activities.detail: Fish for rainbow and brown trout in New Zealand's largest lake, either from the shore or by hiring a guided charter boat.
Hike this world-renowned 19.4 km alpine trail featuring volcanic landscapes, emerald lakes, and panoramic views of three active volcanoes.
Experience an exhilarating jet boat ride through the narrow canyon of the Aratiatia Rapids, passing under natural rock formations.
Explore this stunning geothermal wonderland with hot springs, geysers, and colorful silica terraces accessed by a scenic boat ride.
Take a short, scenic walk to view the dramatic Huka Falls, where the Waikato River plunges 11 metres through a narrow canyon.
Experience adrenaline-pumping activities including bungy jumping from the Taupo Bungy Bridge or skydiving with views over the lake and volcanoes.
Ride the extensive network of trails in the Whakamoenga Regional Park and surrounding areas, ranging from beginner to advanced difficulty.

food.detail: Sample fresh-smoked rainbow or brown trout, a local specialty prepared in various ways at lakeside eateries.
Enjoy premium lamb dishes sourced from the surrounding Waikato region, known for producing some of New Zealand's finest lamb.
Try corn grown in soil warmed by geothermal heat from the volcanic landscape, available at local markets and restaurants.
Sample wines from nearby Central Otago and Marlborough vineyards, particularly crisp Sauvignon Blancs that pair well with local cuisine.

culture.detail: Show respect for Maori cultural sites and traditions; many geothermal areas have spiritual significance to Ngati Tuwharetoa, the local iwi.
Taupo embraces outdoor adventure as a way of life; locals are accustomed to visitors seeking thrilling activities and can offer advice on conditions and safety.
Always stay on designated paths in geothermal areas, never assume hot pools are safe to enter, and heed all warning signs due to unpredictable thermal activity.

places.name: Tokoroa
places.description: An industrial town in the Waikato's south, Tokoroa is surrounded by extensive pine forests and offers access to outdoor activities like mountain biking and hiking. It serves as a practical base for exploring the southern Waikato region.

activities.detail: A purpose-built facility with trails ranging from beginner to advanced, featuring purpose-designed jumps and technical sections through native bush.
A scenic walking trail through the surrounding pine and native forest plantations offering peaceful nature immersion and bird watching opportunities.
A nearby recreational reserve with walking trails, picnic areas, and access to the Putaruru Stream for a half-day nature visit.
Several access points along the Waikato River for fishing, kayaking, and riverside walks in the surrounding district.
Extensive logging company trails through managed plantation forests accessible to hikers and nature enthusiasts exploring the industrial forestry landscape.

food.detail: The region's signature meat product, available at local butchers and restaurants, known for its tender quality from the fertile pastoral lands.
Fresh milk, cheese, and butter sourced from Waikato's extensive dairy farming operations, available at local shops and markets.
A staple espresso-based coffee served at local cafés that reflects New Zealand's strong coffee tradition and relaxed social atmosphere.
Fresh local takeaway featuring fish caught from nearby rivers and regional suppliers, a casual dining standard in small Waikato towns.

culture.detail: Tokoroa's identity centers on forestry and milling industries, visible in the town's infrastructure and employment, so respect active work zones and logging operations.
The local community values conservation and trail maintenance, so follow Leave No Trace principles and report any trail damage to local departments.
The Waikato region has significant Māori heritage and ongoing cultural presence; be respectful of tapu (sacred) sites and seek local guidance when visiting culturally significant areas.
Small-town New Zealand culture emphasizes friendly, direct communication; locals appreciate genuine interest in the area's forestry heritage and outdoor lifestyle.

restaurants.name: Barrington's Restaurant
restaurants.description: Sophisticated fine dining venue showcasing locally-sourced ingredients with creative contemporary dishes.

restaurants.name: The Ivy Restaurant
restaurants.description: Award-winning fine dining establishment offering refined European cuisine in an elegant historic setting.

restaurants.name: Fedora Café & Bar
restaurants.description: Casual all-day dining spot popular for quality brunch, coffee, and relaxed lunch fare.

restaurants.name: Chim Choo Ree
restaurants.description: Authentic Thai restaurant delivering vibrant curries and stir-fries with genuine Southeast Asian flavours.

restaurants.name: The Waikato Tavern
restaurants.description: Historic pub offering hearty traditional fare and craft beers in a welcoming neighbourhood atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Frost Gelato
restaurants.description: Authentic Italian gelato shop crafting small-batch gelato with premium ingredients and creative seasonal flavours.

restaurants.name: The Urban Depot
restaurants.description: Trendy inner-city café serving excellent coffee, innovative brunch dishes, and artisanal baked goods.

restaurants.name: Kintore Street Diner
restaurants.description: Contemporary diner-style restaurant featuring quality burgers, steaks, and comfort food with craft cocktails.

restaurants.name: Hammerhead
restaurants.description: Contemporary bistro offering seasonal menus showcasing local produce with refined but approachable cuisine.

restaurants.name: Noodle House
restaurants.description: Lively noodle bar serving Vietnamese, Thai, and Malaysian noodle dishes with vibrant Asian flavours.

restaurants.name: Café Sanctuary
restaurants.description: Charming small-town café delivering quality coffee, homemade baking, and contemporary lunch options.

restaurants.name: Moxie Restaurant & Bar
restaurants.description: Vibrant contemporary restaurant offering modern small plates, quality wine list, and creative cocktails.

restaurants.name: The Wee Thai Kitchen
restaurants.description: Authentic small Thai restaurant delivering genuine curries and stir-fries in a friendly casual setting.

restaurants.name: Derby Street Kitchen
restaurants.description: Contemporary neighbourhood restaurant showcasing regional ingredients with innovative seasonal cooking.

restaurants.name: Barossa Café
restaurants.description: Regional café offering quality coffee, Mediterranean-inspired lunch dishes, and locally-baked provisions.
